comments.text: One of those problems that, If solved, would simpify some other proof. This is similar in spirit to Feit-Thompson conjecture. It is not surprising that both lack solutions.

Let $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be the set of numbers that are NOT of the form $np^k(p + a_1)^{r_1}(p + a_2)^{r_2}\cdots (p + a_t)^{r_t}$, where $0 < a_1 < \cdots < a_t$, where $k \geq 1$ and each $r_i \geq 0$, where $p$ and each $p + a_i$ are prime, and where \begin{equation*} p > \left(\sum_{0 \leq s_i \leq r_i} a_1^{s_1}\cdots a_t^{s_t}\right)\sigma(n).\end{equation*} Then $A \subseteq S$. To prove this, it suffices to prove that if $m$ is a pseudoperfect number of the above form, then $m$ has a proper factor that is also pseudoperfect. If $m$ is pseudoperfect of the above form, then there exist distinct proper factors $d_i$ of $m$ such that \begin{equation*} d_1 + \cdots + d_l = m. \end{equation*} Since $m$ is a multiple of $p$, the sum $d_1 + \cdots + d_l$ must be a multiple of $p$ as well. For every possible $(p+1)$-rough component (denoted by exponents $s_1, ..., s_t$), consider the sum of all $d_i$ with that component. If at least one of these $d_i$ is not a multiple of $p$, then the sum $\pmod{p}$ is at least 1 and at most $a_1^{s_1}\cdots a_t^{s_t}\sigma(n)$. So if any $d_i$ is not a multiple of $p$, then we have \begin{equation*} p \leq \left(\sum_{0 \leq s_i \leq r_i} a_1^{s_1}\cdots a_t^{s_t}\right)\sigma(n),\end{equation*} a contradiction. So every $d_i$ is a multiple of $p$, meaning that $\frac{m}{p}$ is also pseudoperfect. This proves that $A \subseteq S$. I don't know how helpful this is (in particular, I don't know anything about the asymptotic behavior of $S$, though I haven't really looked into it too closely), but I'm leaving this note here just in case it may be useful.
